title: Window-cut page apertures
materials: cartridge-paper-220 (for the backing page behind the window)
sources: SYNTHESISED — window-cut page design is a contemporary journalling craft technique. No pre-1928 PD source. Draws on bookbinding and paper engineering principles.
notes: Cutting rectangular or shaped apertures into a journal page so that the page beneath is visible through the window. The page beneath becomes part of the design of the page above. Covers: planning the window placement (a light pencil template), cutting cleanly through a single page only (the trick: cut on a mat that gives slight resistance, not on the underlying pages), four-corner entry cuts for rectangular windows vs free-cut for organic shapes, reinforcing the window edges with a thin washi tape frame if the paper is thin, using the underlying page colour or image as a design element. Also: stacking two windows on consecutive pages for depth.
